title: Fuel-efficient driving technique
summary: How driving style affects fuel use, range and emissions.

sections.heading: Smooth acceleration and gear changes
sections.body: Accelerate firmly but smoothly and change up early — around 2,000 rpm for diesel and 2,500 rpm for petrol. Pulling away in a low gear and over-revving wastes fuel without saving time.

sections.heading: Steady speed and look ahead
sections.body: A constant speed uses less fuel than constant accelerating and braking. Look far ahead so you can ease off the throttle in time and let the engine slow the car (motorrem).

sections.heading: Speed and aerodynamics
sections.body: Air resistance grows sharply with speed. Driving 100 km/h instead of 120 km/h on the motorway can save 10–20% fuel. Remove unused roof boxes and bike racks when not in use.

title: Vehicle care that saves fuel
summary: How maintenance and weight choices keep emissions and costs down.

sections.heading: Tyre pressure and condition
sections.body: Under-inflated tyres increase rolling resistance and fuel use, and wear faster. Check pressure cold once a month and before long trips. The correct value is on the door pillar or fuel cap.

sections.heading: Weight and load
sections.body: Every extra kilo costs fuel. Empty the boot of things you do not need. A full roof rack can add up to 20% to fuel consumption at motorway speed.

sections.heading: Maintenance
sections.body: A blocked air filter, worn spark plugs or dirty injectors all raise fuel use and emissions. Keep service intervals and use the recommended grade of oil — a thinner specified oil reduces internal friction.

title: Electric and hybrid driving
summary: Specifics for EVs and hybrids: regeneration, range and charging.

sections.heading: Regenerative braking
sections.body: Electric and hybrid cars recover energy when you lift off or brake gently. Anticipate further ahead and brake earlier and more gently to maximise regeneration; harsh braking still wastes energy as heat.

sections.heading: Range and temperature
sections.body: Cold weather, motorway speeds and heating reduce EV range significantly. Pre-conditioning the cabin while still plugged in saves driving range. Plan charging stops on long trips.

sections.heading: Charging on public roads
sections.body: Public charging bays are reserved for vehicles that are actually charging. Move the car promptly when charging is finished. Cables must not cross the footway in a way that could trip pedestrians.

title: Sharing the road and the environment
summary: How considerate driving reduces stress, danger and pollution.

sections.heading: Idling and stop-start
sections.body: Idling for more than about 30 seconds uses more fuel than restarting the engine. Modern cars have stop-start systems that do this automatically. In residential streets, switch off the engine while waiting.

sections.heading: Sharing space with cyclists and pedestrians
sections.body: In built-up areas, much of the traffic is on bikes and on foot. Drive at a calm pace, leave room and accept losing a few seconds rather than forcing your way through. Cleaner, quieter driving also benefits everyone living along the street.

sections.heading: Environmental zones and low-emission choices
sections.body: Older, more polluting vehicles are excluded from environmental zones in major cities. Choosing a cleaner car, car-sharing or combining trips reduces both costs and emissions, and keeps city air liveable.
